channel.title: Ajahn Funn Ācāro Quotes
channel.description: Dhamma quotes by Ajahn Funn Ācāro, updated every 24 hours

item.title: Ajahn Funn: When we abstain from them, wherever we go we are peaceful, happy, content, and at ease. Thus, when we undertake the precepts, the…
item.description:
Wherever We Go, We Are Peaceful Because We Have No Faults

When we abstain from them, wherever we go we are peaceful, happy, content, and at ease. Thus, when we undertake the precepts, the Buddha says: Sīlena sugatiṃ yanti – "By virtue one goes to a happy destiny." Those who have abstained are happy because they have abstained from these faults – they have no faults. Nowadays, why are people poor? They are not poor for any other reason. They are poor because of thievery like this. They are poor because they fear being killed, struck, pierced, or stabbed like this. They fear their children or spouse being unfaithful, or they fear being cheated or deceived, or they fear drunken people. These fears are what make them poor. Why do we build houses and rooms with locks and keys? Because of thieves. If there were no thieves, it would be delightful and at ease. We could go anywhere, leave our things anywhere, and there would be no bandits, no thieves, no nothing. Then we would be at ease. That is how it is. The Buddha taught this. These are things people do not want. By abandoning them we have happiness, because we have no pāpa (evil), no kamma of those five types – no such faults. Then we have happiness. Wherever we go, we are happy. Whatever we do, we are happy.

item.title: Ajahn Funn: Now, as for adinnādāna (taking what is not given): wherever we go, we fear thieves. We fear thieves, so we abstain. If everyone…
item.description:
The Results of Theft and Sexual Misconduct: Why We Abstain

Now, as for adinnādāna (taking what is not given): wherever we go, we fear thieves. We fear thieves, so we abstain. If everyone abstains, then there will be no thieves. We can leave our belongings anywhere and be at ease. Houses wouldn’t need locks and keys. We don’t desire or want that. If we steal from others, those karmic results follow us, in this life and future lives. Know this well. If we don’t steal, then no such kamma exists. If we do steal, that kamma bears fruit: in whatever birth or realm we are born, there will be only thieves, robbers, plunderers, or we will be punished and fined by authorities, or attacked by bandits, or suffer floods, or be blown away by storms. We don’t want that, we don’t desire it. Therefore, the Buddha taught adinnādānā veramaṇī – abstain from taking what is not given.

Now, regarding sexual misconduct (kāmesu micchācāra): Why? So that we can have a family, get married, live together, have a wife or husband, and be faithful to each other – then we are at ease. If we have a wife and have to constantly fear that she will go with another man, we don’t want that. Or if we have a husband who goes with another woman, we don’t want that. Think about it. Or if we have children and someone harms them, we don’t want that. Or whoever wants it? It’s ourselves. If we have precepts, and we respect others’ children, wives, and husbands, we don’t harm each other, and we live happily.

item.title: Ajahn Funn: In the old days, when Phra Ajaan Mun had a hut, he’d tear out the floor and make it a walking meditation path.…
item.description:
Phra Ajaan Mun’s Effort: No Hut, Just a Walking Path and a Platform

In the old days, when Phra Ajaan Mun had a hut, he’d tear out the floor and make it a walking meditation path. He’d make a platform like this sermon seat and put his sleeping mat and pillow there. That’s where he’d spend the night. When he was tired from walking, he’d stand. Tired from standing, he’d sit. Tired from sitting, he’d walk. He exerted that much effort. That’s how he became our teacher. He did this for 16 years, and when he came to know, that’s how he was able to teach us all, up to the present day.

Before him, there was no one to teach these things. They said that the Buddha’s time was past. They had a lot of merit but hadn’t studied much, hadn’t meditated much. They’d simply chant the names of the 40 meditation themes, starting with the ten recollections, the ten kasiṇas, the ten contemplations of unattractiveness, the four formless jhānas, the four sublime attitudes (brahma-vihāra), the analysis into elements, and the loathsomeness of food. They’d repeat the 40 meditation themes—how many times? They’d take 108 sticks of incense and count until they had reached all 108. That’s how they used to meditate. If you wanted to do walking meditation, you had to do that first. But nowadays, we don’t do things that way. We can meditate in all our activities, in every posture. That’s the way it is.

item.title: Ajahn Funn: This Dhamma is the sole sovereign (ekarāja). The Dhamma-king is supreme. The one who practices Dhamma sees it in themselves: sanditthiko. How is…
item.description:
The Ocean Never Says It Is the Ocean; We Are the Ones Who Assume

This Dhamma is the sole sovereign (ekarāja). The Dhamma-king is supreme. The one who practices Dhamma sees it in themselves: sanditthiko. How is that? We go around trying to receive things. Seeing others say it’s good, we say it’s good too. When evil arises, we say it’s evil along with them. But that thing itself never says it’s good or bad. The ocean never says it’s the ocean. Ask it, “Are you the ocean?” Silence. It never says it’s anything at all. We are the ones who say it. Ask a mountain, “Are you a mountain?” Silence. It doesn’t say anything. “Are you earth?” Silence. It doesn’t say anything. It is completely neutral. We are the ones who assume and label.

This is how beings are sunk in the great ocean — sunk in conventions and opinions (sammuti-niyāma). If others say it’s good, we go along with their good. If they say it’s bad, we go along with their bad. If they say it’s suffering, we suffer along with them. Don’t be deluded. Know the conditioned phenomena thoroughly. If others say it’s good but we are not good, what can they say? If others say it’s bad but we are not bad, what can they say? If they say it’s pleasant but we are not pleasant, what can they say? If they say it’s suffering but we are not suffering, what can they say? That is being the sole sovereign, supreme, unshaken like this. This is knowing the aggregate of conditioned phenomena thoroughly.

item.title: Ajahn Funn: When the mind is not good, know it and think “Buddho, Buddho” – cut it off. Now, what about happiness and prosperity? Our…
item.description:
When the Mind Is Good, Everything Is Good – Happiness and Prosperity Follow

When the mind is not good, know it and think “Buddho, Buddho” – cut it off. Now, what about happiness and prosperity? Our mind is good, happy and at ease, cool-hearted, free from suffering, heat, turmoil, and trouble. This produces happiness and prosperity. When our mind is good, everything we do is good. Business is good, livelihood is good, study is good, whatever we do is good. Family is good, neighbors, marketplace, and the nation are good. It is like this. Let everyone come to know. When our mind is good and thinks good, we are happy. Sitting is happy, lying down is happy, walking is happy, standing is happy. The mind is happy, the mind is at ease. When the mind is at ease, everything you do is easy, everything you seek is easy, everything you take is easy, business is easy, government service is easy, family is easy, parents and siblings are easy, neighbors and marketplace are easy, the nation is easy. Look – what do you say, all of us?

item.title: Ajahn Funn: Rice, water, food – they don't tell us to eat them. Do they? Does rice say, "If you don't eat me, I'll hit…
item.description:
Rice and Money Are Neutral – We Are the Ones Who Want Them

Rice, water, food – they don't tell us to eat them. Do they? Does rice say, "If you don't eat me, I'll hit your mouth"? Rice is neutral, isn't it? Does money say, "If you don't keep me, I'll hit your hand"? It doesn't say anything. It remains neutral. Left in the sun and rain, it stays like that. That's how it is. But we want it ourselves. If we want it, then we must be diligent and industrious. But let the mind be happy, let the mind be at ease.

When the mind is happy and at ease, everything is at ease. That's the important point. Has your tiredness gone? If you want to sleep, sleep while focusing on the heart until you fall asleep. When the mind has Buddho, it is greater than everything. Greater than Indra, Brahma, devaputras, devas, yakkhas, kumbhāṇḍas, garuḍas, nāgas. When has Buddho ever been insignificant? Our mind is greater than all of them. That's how it is. Do you understand? Alright, we can stop.

item.title: Ajahn Funn: We have taken it to be a self, a being, a person, a me, a him. When we contemplate the basic kammaṭṭhāna and…
item.description:
The 32 Parts: Bile, Phlegm, Pus, Blood – Where Is the Person Here?

We have taken it to be a self, a being, a person, a me, a him. When we contemplate the basic kammaṭṭhāna and see that it is not a being, not a person, not a self, not a soul, not me, not him – why is that said? Let us contemplate. Raise it up like this: pittaṃ (bile), semhaṃ (phlegm), pubbo (pus), lohitaṃ (blood), sedo (sweat), medo (fat), assu (tears), vasā (oil), kheḷo (saliva), siṅghāṇikā (snot), lasikā (synovial fluid), muttaṃ (urine). Where is the person here? None of this is woman or man, no one at all. It is simply that.

When we see this, we can abandon sakkāyadiṭṭhi (personality belief). As for vicikicchā (doubt), the uncertainty whether it might be this or that – there is none. It is truly that way. Seeing that, our mind becomes empty, calms down. The matter is just like this. We can see it clearly. For example, how could snot be a person? How could saliva be a person? How could urine be a person? Contemplate and see. When we see clearly, truly, directly like that, we see that there is no self, no being, no person, no me, no him. Those things are not anything; they simply exist that way. We have been deluded, grasping them as a self, as a soul.

item.title: Ajahn Funn: The other extreme that you, as renunciants, should not pursue is hate — the fire of dosa. When the fire of dosa ignites…
item.description:
The Fire of Anger (Dosa) Also Burns – Avoid Both Extremes

The other extreme that you, as renunciants, should not pursue is hate — the fire of dosa. When the fire of dosa ignites in the mind-continuum (citta-santāna) of anyone, it burns that person, causing them to think maliciously and do evil, base deeds. They have no hiri and ottappa — no shame or fear of wrongdoing. That evil they have done brings suffering, harm, and danger, causing mental distress upon themselves. This is the result of the extreme that monks should not pursue — that is, hate. As for not loving and not hating — that is the majjhimāpaṭipadā, the middle way of practice that monks should all follow and observe. This middle way, this paṭipadā, is a path free from peril and danger. Just as when we travel, if we step to the left we encounter only thorns and brambles, and if we step to the right we also encounter thorns. Or like a car that leaves the road onto the left shoulder — it gets stuck and overturns; if it goes onto the right shoulder — it also falls and overturns, causing danger and damage to itself. If the car stays right on the road without veering off, it can travel comfortably and safely reach its destination.

item.title: Ajahn Funn: Note this well. Alright, today listen to the discourse. Have you had enough of listening to discourses? You all already know. You already…
item.description:
Buddho, Buddho – The Knower in the Heart, Our Refuge and Abode

Note this well.

Alright, today listen to the discourse. Have you had enough of listening to discourses? You all already know. You already know everything, don't you? Birth, aging, sickness, death you already know, don't you? Happiness and suffering you already know, don't you? Or not? We have seen it all. From now on, listen to Dhamma. Listening to Dhamma has no end. Come, take your place to listen to Dhamma. Sit comfortably, sit cross-legged with right leg over left, right hand over left if you like. When your mind is at ease, recollect your refuge. What is our refuge? The Buddha, Dhamma, Saṅgha. Believe that the Buddha is in the heart, the Dhamma is in the heart, the Noble Saṅgha is in the heart. Why say they are in the heart? Buddha — knowing — is in the heart, not anywhere else.

Believe firmly like that, then recite and meditate like this: Buddho, Dhammo, Saṅgho, Buddho, Dhammo, Saṅgho, Buddho, Dhammo, Saṅgho three times. Then combine into just the single word Buddho, Buddho. Close your eyes, shut your mouth. The tongue does not move. Recollect within the heart. Where does the feeling of Buddho know? Recollect and see. Buddho — wherever it feels, set up mindfulness right there to watch that knowing.

item.title: Ajahn Funn: When our body is comfortable, recite or recollect: Buddho, Dhammo, Saṅgho three times, then settle on just Buddho, Buddho as a single word.…
item.description:
Establish the Mind in Buddho: The Knower Is Not Subject to Destruction

When our body is comfortable, recite or recollect: Buddho, Dhammo, Saṅgho three times, then settle on just Buddho, Buddho as a single word. Don't send your mind forward, backward, left, right, up, or down. Establish it right in the middle, at the place of knowing. Buddho, Buddho – stay there. Don't send your mind elsewhere. Close your eyes, close your mouth, and focus. Make the mind calm. We want happiness and comfort. Don't think about suffering and difficulty. Whatever is suffering – that is not our self. Don't send the mind out. Establish it at the place of knowing. This "knowing" is important. It is not subject to breaking, destruction, or loss. It is the inner knower. Focus to see that it exists individually for each person. Happiness and suffering, good and bad – all are conventions. Happiness is something we label ourselves. Suffering we label ourselves. Good we label ourselves. Bad we label ourselves. Don't label anything. Cool, hot – we label it all ourselves. Don't label. Just set the mind at ease. That's enough. Then things will be good.
